Use the sticky notes below to brainstorm on your ICP attributes

I’m a serial entrepreneur (4 startups and counting), board member, and product growth exec. I’ve led product teams at UserCentrics, Smallpdf, GotPhoto (EQT), DeinDeal, and Jua.ai.

I specialize in B2B scaleups, particularly those navigating the chaos of product-led growth (PLG), and I advise companies like Toggl, Paddle, and NorthOne. I also work as a portfolio advisor for Notion Capital, focusing on European Series A+ SaaS and Cloud startups.

Has at least a budget of 10,000 USD
A good Ideal Customer Profile is not describing all of your customers but only a fraction, those that you want to have more of.
In general these would be customers that derive the MOST value from you, while you get the MOST value from them.
Brainstorm with sticky notes what is your ideal Customer
Add one characteristic per post it about your ICP and make sure to have between anywhere 5 - 20 before you continue.

ICP Missing Categories: Recommendations

This document provides recommendations for completing the five missing categories in your Ideal Customer Profile definition for a website creation product targeting non-technical solopreneurs.

Tools

Why This Matters

Without knowing current tools, you cannot identify integration opportunities, competitive displacement strategies, or workflow pain points. Understanding what solopreneurs already use reveals where they're experiencing friction and what "jobs" remain unsolved—critical for positioning your solution effectively.

Where to Find This Information

  1. Customer interviews (primary source): Ask 10-15 current or prospective customers: "Walk me through your current workflow for managing your business online" and "What tools do you currently pay for?"
  2. Quantitative validation: Survey your email list or early users asking them to select from a list of common tools (Canva, Squarespace, WordPress, social media schedulers, etc.)
  3. Tool usage data: If you have existing users, track what services they mention connecting or migrating from during onboarding

Industries

Why This Matters

Industry specificity determines messaging effectiveness, feature prioritization, and go-to-market strategy. A fitness coach has different website needs than an e-commerce seller. Without this, you risk building generic features that don't deeply serve anyone, making your product harder to position and sell.

Where to Find This Information

  1. Analyze existing customer base: If you have any users, categorize them by industry. Look for patterns in who converts fastest and has highest retention
  2. Customer discovery interviews: Ask prospects "What type of business are you starting?" Document answers across 20+ conversations to identify clusters
  3. Digital ethnography: Join solopreneur communities (Reddit's r/solopreneur, Indie Hackers, niche Facebook groups) and observe what types of businesses people discuss launching most frequently

Champions

Why This Matters

For solopreneurs, the champion IS the buyer, but you need to understand their network influence. Champions determine how your product spreads through word-of-mouth—a critical acquisition channel for low-cost products. Knowing if they're active in communities, social media, or peer networks shapes your viral mechanics and referral strategy.

Where to Find This Information

  1. Behavioral interviews: Ask existing users "How did you first hear about website builders?" and "Where do you go for business advice?" to map their information networks
  2. Social listening: Track where solopreneurs discuss website tools (Twitter/X, LinkedIn, YouTube comments on business channels)
  3. Referral source analysis: Tag and track how new users find you—if significant traffic comes from communities or content creators, those individuals are your champions

Business Model

Why This Matters

Revenue model impacts purchasing behavior, budget allocation, and product requirements. A solopreneur running a service business has different cash flow patterns than someone selling digital products. This affects pricing strategy, payment terms, and which features deliver immediate revenue impact (crucial for retention).

Where to Find This Information

  1. Direct questioning in interviews: "How do you plan to make money?" and "When do you expect your first revenue?" reveals monetization timelines
  2. Onboarding questionnaires: Add business model selection during signup (services, e-commerce, affiliate, content/ads, digital products)
  3. Cohort analysis: Track which business models have highest activation rates and lowest churn—this indicates product-market fit strength per segment

Buyer Personas

Why This Matters

Even within solopreneurs, decision-making psychology varies significantly. A former corporate employee launching a consulting business makes different buying decisions than a young creator monetizing a passion project. Personas drive messaging, channel selection, and objection handling—without them, marketing becomes guesswork.

Where to Find This Information

  1. Jobs-to-be-Done interviews: Conduct 15-20 interviews asking "What were you doing when you decided you needed a website?" and "What alternatives did you consider?" to uncover decision-making contexts
  2. Demographic + psychographic surveys: Collect age, background, risk tolerance, and decision criteria from your user base
  3. Win/loss analysis: Interview both customers who bought and prospects who didn't. Ask "What almost stopped you from buying?" to identify persona-specific concerns

Implementation Priority: Start with customer interviews across all categories simultaneously. Schedule 15-20 conversations asking open-ended questions about tools, industries, business models, and decision processes. Quantitative data follows to validate patterns from qualitative insights.
